channel.title: Writing on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Writing on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Evolution Of Design Documentation
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the evolution of design documentation.

This actually comes from a question from Noah Stavish, who asked: “Hey Tim, can you discuss what you expect out of design and technical documentation, both when you write it and when you review what others have written? Can you also touch on how teamwide expectations for documentation has changed over the past 40 years? What problems and solutions have you faced with these expectations?

item.title: Writing My Own TTRPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing my own TBL toop roleplaying game.

I’m actually answering a question from all Lucky Seven, who asked: would you like to write a pen and paper RPG? If so, considering your knowledge of computer game design, how would you approach it? What do you think you would make differently from someone who’s never done a crpg?

That’s an interesting question I’ve had. I’ve seen other people ask me like, hey, why don’t you make a tabletop RPG or make a pen and paper RPG? And I’m always like, eh, but this question made me think, oh yeah, making computer RPGs is kind of a different design space than tabletop, and that is an interesting question to me. So let me answer the base question right off the bat, which is, yes, I have thought off and on, but never seriously, about making a tabletop RPG. I think that, having made a bunch of computer RPGs and they’re different, and I’ll talk about that — I think I would have, based on that experience, quite a number of advantages and a few disadvantages if I tried to make a pen and P, pen and pencil, paper and pencil RPG.

item.title: My Rejected Star Trek TNG Script
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my rejected Star Trek next Generation script.

This is a question from Wes 9353, who asked: have you talked more about the Star Trek TNG episode that you co-wrote, or was that just on the podcast he’s referring to was an interview I did with pistol shrimp game and I’ll put the link below, and that was a fun talk. It was the erquan Masters guys. It was super fun. I encourage you to watch it — about 22 minutes in. I do mention that I co-wrote a script, a screenplay, for Star Trek next Generation. It got rejected, but let me talk to you about it, like how it came to be, what the plot was. And I thought it was a pretty good plot, was a very star tracki in plot.

item.title: My Memoirs, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my Memoirs again, so consider this a my Memoirs video, part two.

So I’ve shown it before. It’s this big honken thing I wrote and got printed, sent to some people for feedback in that first video that I link below. You can watch it to learn why I wrote it, what’s inside and why I’m not going to publish it. So if you have questions about any of that, um, watch the first video, because I’m not going to talk about that here. But this Memoir and this channel has given me a lot of feedback on me and I’ve learned a lot, and these are things that are interesting and I’ll talk about a lot of them. But I want to really talk about the fact that, while I’ve gotten a lot of wonderful feedback, really nice things — especially some nice things from people I’ve worked with who could have said nothing that I haven’t talked to in 10, 15, in one case I heard from someone I haven’t talked to in, haven’t talked to, I haven’t talked to in 27 years, but they wanted to know that they liked working with me, which is really really sweet and really nice. But I’ve gotten a lot of negative feedback too, and I’ve gotten that negative feedback from people who meant it constructively and said: oh yeah, I loved working with You know you do this thing, though sometimes, and other people they were just angry and like, ah, you do this blah. But I heard it enough from both kinds of people that I’m like this stuff has got to be true. So let me tell you what the negative feedback I’ve gotten about me.

item.title: Understanding Someone Else’s POV
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about understanding someone else’s point of view.

Now this is going to sound like an odd topic and I will end the video on why I’m telling you about this on a game development Channel, but I want to talk about it because it’s something I’ve always liked doing and it gets me into trouble a lot, and I’m going to talk to you about why that is so. Understanding someone else’s point of view is something I really like doing. I like asking people: why do you like that game or why do you hate that game or movie or sometimes, political candidate, although on that one I often just back off and go. I don’t think people even know why they like the political candidate they do.

item.title: Being A Good Storyteller
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about being a good story teller.

This is actually a response to a question from Arda Osler 631, who asks: could we get a video going over how you got to be such a good Storyteller, if that’s the appropriate term? First off, Arta, thank you, that’s really sweet to say. Um, normally I guess I wouldn’t do a whole video on this, but it’s Friday fun day, so let’s have fun. Um, at least I’ll have fun. Talk about me, okay, so I have heard that for a long time I’ve had people tell me I tell good stories. I got it in, you know, high school and college during gaming sessions. I got it a lot.

item.title: Writing Evil Characters
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing evil characters.

Specifically, I want to answer a question, a second question, uh, from Nash kruka, who asks: how, where do you find yourself wanting to draw the line for evil options in an RPG? Similarly to how I find myself having a hard time choosing the evil option in a game, I find myself having a hard time writing them? Most games I’ve played have far fewer Bad Karma options than good. Maybe one evil dialogue to the half dozen good dialogue options. How do you weigh in on this? Okay, Nash, I know you’re asking about how to write dialogue for the player where it’s evil. Um, the way I think about this is: how would I write a good evil NPC?

item.title: Writing Good NPCs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing good NPCs.

Now I’m going to start with the big caveat that I’ve said on here many times: I am not a good writer. I’ve tried to be good, I’ve worked on It’s something I can improve past a certain limited amount of. I can write lore text. I can write short narration, but personal one-on-one dialogue — no, I’m not that good at, but I am good at seeing good writing of that category.

item.title: Writing Design Specifications
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about how to write design specifications.

Specifically, I’m going to answer a question from Thor RoR TV who asks: I was trying to write a design document for a solo project I’m working on and was wondering if you had any examples of how to properly write a specification that might be given to a programmer or an artist. Also, are there any pitfalls to avoid when writing speci ifications? That is a very good question, thoro TV, and it’s also a little hard to answer because there’s a wild, wildly huge range of design specifications. You might be writing a might be writing, you might be writing a system mechanic. You might be writing a user interface, you might be writing a — um, some narrative guidelines.

item.title: My Memoirs
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to revisit something I mentioned in a previous video, where I talked about my notes, and I’ll link that below.

I want to talk specifically about my book, this big, thick hardback I wrote during the pandemic: I — it’s basically my career. I don’t know if I call it my autobiography or my Memoirs, not exactly sure what the difference is between those. But after moving up to Seattle and not knowing anybody — and it was the middle of the pandemic and everything was shut down — I decided to collect all my notes, whether they were physical or digital. I digitized a lot of the physical ones and then I started organizing them by company and, inside, by that, by product. To give you an idea — and I think I’ve mentioned this somewhere else — um, my Fallout notes alone, digitally, or 86 pages long, and that’s not a page per note. That’s the notes, basically: sentence, paragraph, sentence.
